sentences.chinese: 淮南厲王長，高帝少子也，其母故趙王張敖美人。
sentences.literal: The Prince Li of Huainan, Liu Chang, was a younger son of the Supreme Emperor; his mother had been a beauty of King Zhang Ao of Zhao.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu Chang, posthumously known as the Prince Li of Huainan, was one of the Gaozu Emperor's younger sons. His mother had been a concubine of Zhang Ao, the king of Zhao.

sentences.chinese: 高帝八年，從東垣過趙，趙王獻美人，厲王母也，幸，有身。
sentences.literal: In the eighth year of the Supreme Emperor, passing through Zhao from Dongyuan, the King of Zhao presented a beauty—she was the mother of Prince Li—who received favor and became pregnant.
sentences.idiomatic: In 199 BCE, while returning from Dongyuan through Zhao, the king of Zhao offered him a woman who became Prince Li's mother. She entered the emperor's favor and conceived.

sentences.chinese: 趙王不敢內宮，為築外宮捨之。
sentences.literal: The King of Zhao did not dare bring her into the inner palace; he built an outer palace and lodged her there.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Zhao dared not house her in his inner quarters, so he had a separate residence built outside the palace and kept her there.

sentences.chinese: 及貫高等謀反事覺，並逮治王，盡捕王母兄弟美人，系之河內。
sentences.literal: When the plotting to rebel by Guan Gao and others was discovered, together they seized and prosecuted the king, capturing all the king's mother, brothers, and beauties, and bound them in Henei.
sentences.idiomatic: When Guan Gao's plot to assassinate the emperor came to light, the king of Zhao was arrested along with everyone connected to him—his mother, his brothers, his concubines—and they were all held in custody in Henei.

sentences.chinese: 厲王母亦系，告吏曰：「日得幸上，有子。」
sentences.literal: The mother of Prince Li was also bound; she told the clerks, saying, "I once received favor from the Supreme One; I have a son."
sentences.idiomatic: Prince Li's mother was among the prisoners. She told the officials, "The emperor favored me once—I am carrying his child."

sentences.chinese: 吏以聞，上方怒趙，未及理厲王母。
sentences.literal: The clerks reported it; the Supreme One was then angry at Zhao and had not yet reached to dealing with the mother of Prince Li.
sentences.idiomatic: They passed the word upward, but Gaozu was still furious with Zhao and had no attention to spare for her case.

sentences.chinese: 厲王母弟趙兼因辟陽侯言呂後，呂後妒，不肯白，辟陽侯不強爭。
sentences.literal: The younger brother of the mother of Prince Li, Zhao Jian, through the Marquis of Piyang spoke to Empress Lü; Empress Lü was jealous and was unwilling to speak plainly; the Marquis of Piyang did not forcefully contend.
sentences.idiomatic: Her brother Zhao Jian appealed through the Marquis of Piyang to Empress Lü, but Lü was jealous and refused to put in a word for her. The marquis did not press the matter.

sentences.chinese: 厲王母已生厲生，恚，即自殺。
sentences.literal: The mother of Prince Li had already given birth to him; resentful, she thereupon killed herself.
sentences.idiomatic: Once she had delivered the child who would later be enfeoffed as Prince Li of Huainan, she killed herself in bitterness and despair.

sentences.chinese: 吏奉厲王詣上，上悔，令呂後母之，而葬其母真定。
sentences.literal: The clerks presented Prince Li to the Supreme One; the Supreme One regretted it, ordered Empress Lü to mother him, and buried his mother at Zhending.
sentences.idiomatic: Officials brought the infant to the emperor, who was stricken with remorse. He had Empress Lü raise the child as her own and had the mother buried in Zhending.

sentences.chinese: 真定，厲王母家縣也。
sentences.literal: Zhending was the home county of the mother of Prince Li.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhending was her family's home county.

sentences.chinese: 十一年，淮南王布反，上自將擊滅布，即立子長為淮南子。
sentences.literal: In the eleventh year, the King of Huainan, Bu, rebelled; the Supreme One personally led troops, struck and destroyed Bu, and thereupon established his son Chang as Prince of Huainan.
sentences.idiomatic: In 196 BCE, when Ying Bu, king of Huainan, rose in revolt, Gaozu led the army in person, crushed him, and enfeoffed his son Liu Chang as the new king of Huainan.

sentences.chinese: 王早失母，常附呂後，孝惠、呂後時以故得幸無患，然常心怨辟陽侯，不敢發。
sentences.literal: The king had early lost his mother, constantly attached himself to Empress Lü, and in the time of Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Lü for this reason received favor without calamity; yet constantly in his heart he resented the Marquis of Piyang, not daring to give vent.
sentences.idiomatic: Orphaned young, he had grown up under Empress Lü's wing and was spared during the reigns of Emperor Hui and the Lü empresses, but he never forgave the Marquis of Piyang—only he dared not show it.

sentences.chinese: 及孝文初即位，自以為最親，驕蹇，數不奉法。
sentences.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had first ascended the throne, he considered himself the closest kin, was arrogant and refractory, and repeatedly did not observe the laws.
sentences.idiomatic: Once Emperor Wen took the throne, Liu Chang assumed that as the emperor's only full brother he could do as he pleased. He grew arrogant, flouted the law again and again, and treated the court with contempt.

sentences.chinese: 上寬赦之。
sentences.literal: The Supreme One broadly pardoned him.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor repeatedly overlooked his offenses.

sentences.chinese: 三年，入朝，甚橫。
sentences.literal: In the third year, he entered court audience, very overbearing.
sentences.idiomatic: In the third year of Wen's reign he came to the capital and behaved with shocking arrogance.

sentences.chinese: 從上入苑獵，與上同輦，常謂上「大兄」。
sentences.literal: Following the Supreme One into the park to hunt, riding in the same carriage with the Supreme One, he constantly called the Supreme One "great elder brother."
sentences.idiomatic: He rode beside the emperor in the imperial carriage when they hunted in the park and habitually addressed him as "big brother" instead of as his sovereign.

sentences.chinese: 厲王有材力，力扛鼎，乃往請辟陽侯。
sentences.literal: Prince Li had talent and strength; his strength could shoulder a tripod; he thereupon went to request an audience with the Marquis of Piyang.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu Chang was powerfully built—strong enough to lift a bronze cauldron—and he went to call on the Marquis of Piyang.

sentences.chinese: 辟陽侯出見之，即自袖金椎椎之，命從者刑之。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Piyang came out to see him; he immediately from his sleeve took a metal mallet and mallet-struck him, ordering followers to execute him.
sentences.idiomatic: When the marquis came out to greet him, Liu Chang produced a metal hammer from his sleeve, struck him down, and had his attendants finish the killing.

sentences.chinese: 馳詣闕下，肉袒而謝曰：「臣母不當坐趙時事，辟陽侯力能得之呂後，不爭，罪一也。
sentences.literal: He galloped to below the palace gate, bared his upper body, and apologized, saying, "My mother ought not to have been implicated in the affairs of Zhao; the Marquis of Piyang had strength enough to obtain her release from Empress Lü, yet did not contend—this is his first crime."
sentences.idiomatic: He raced to the palace gate, stripped to the waist in token of submission, and said, "My mother should never have been punished for the Zhao affair. The Marquis of Piyang could have persuaded Empress Lü to spare her, but he would not speak up—that is his first offense against me."

sentences.chinese: 趙王如意子母無罪，呂後殺之，辟陽侯不爭，罪二也。
sentences.literal: The King of Zhao Ruyi, mother and son, were without guilt; Empress Lü killed them; the Marquis of Piyang did not contend—this is his second crime.
sentences.idiomatic: Prince Ruyi of Zhao and his mother were innocent, yet Empress Lü murdered them while the marquis stood silent—that is his second offense.

sentences.chinese: 呂後王諸呂，欲以危劉氏，辟陽侯不爭，罪三也。
sentences.literal: Empress Lü ennobled the Lüs as kings, intending thereby to endanger the house of Liu; the Marquis of Piyang did not contend—this is his third crime.
sentences.idiomatic: When Empress Lü made her kinsmen kings and set out to destroy the Liu house, he raised no objection—that is his third offense.

sentences.chinese: 臣謹為天下誅賊，報母之仇，伏闕下請罪。」
sentences.literal: Your servant respectfully for the realm executed the villain, avenging his mother's wrong; he prostrates below the gate and requests punishment."
sentences.idiomatic: I have rid the empire of a traitor and avenged my mother. I throw myself on your mercy beneath the gate."

sentences.chinese: 文帝傷其志，為親故不治，赦之。
sentences.literal: Emperor Xiaowen was moved by his intent; because of kinship he did not prosecute and pardoned him.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wen pitied the passion behind the act and, unwilling to punish his own brother, let him go.

sentences.chinese: 當是時，自薄太后及太子諸大臣皆憚厲王，厲王以此歸國益恣，不用漢法，出入警蹕，稱制，自作法令，數上書不遜順。
sentences.literal: At this time, from Empress Dowager Bo down to the heir apparent and the great ministers, all feared Prince Li; Prince Li for this reason returning to his state became more wanton, did not use Han law, in going out and in had clearing of the way and prohibition of passage, assumed imperial style, himself made laws and orders, and repeatedly submitted memorials not submissive and compliant.
sentences.idiomatic: From Empress Dowager Bo to the crown prince and the senior ministers, everyone walked in fear of him. Emboldened, he returned to Huainan more lawless than ever: he ignored Han statutes, cleared the roads as an emperor would, issued edicts in his own name, drafted his own laws, and sent memorial after memorial in a tone that brooked no obedience.

sentences.chinese: 文帝重自切責之。
sentences.literal: Emperor Xiaowen repeatedly himself sternly reproached him.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor admonished him sternly, again and again.

sentences.chinese: 時帝舅薄昭為將軍，尊重，上令昭予厲王書諫數之，曰：
sentences.literal: At that time the emperor's maternal uncle Bo Zhao was a general, honored and weighty; the Supreme One ordered Zhao to give Prince Li a letter remonstrating and enumerating his faults, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor turned to his uncle Bo Zhao, a respected general, and had him draft a letter of rebuke listing Liu Chang's transgressions. It read:

sentences.chinese: 竊聞大王剛直而勇，慈惠而厚，貞信多斷，是天以聖人之資奉大王也甚盛，不可不察。
sentences.literal: I have heard in private that the Great King is firm and straight yet brave, kind and gracious yet generous, chaste and trustworthy and much decisive; this is Heaven with the endowment of a sage serving the Great King in very fullness—it cannot but be examined.
sentences.idiomatic: I have heard that Your Highness combines courage with integrity, generosity with kindness, and sound judgment with steadfast honor—gifts Heaven has lavished on you as on a sage. You owe it to yourself to reflect on that.

sentences.chinese: 今大王所行，不稱天資。
sentences.literal: Now what the Great King practices does not match Heaven's endowment.
sentences.idiomatic: What you have been doing of late is unworthy of those gifts.

sentences.chinese: 皇帝初即位，易侯邑在淮南者，大王不肯。
sentences.literal: When the Emperor had first ascended the throne, he shifted the fiefs of marquises located in Huainan; the Great King was unwilling.
sentences.idiomatic: When the emperor first came to the throne, he proposed moving certain marquises' fiefs out of Huainan, and you refused.

sentences.chinese: 皇帝卒易之，使大王得三縣之實，甚厚。
sentences.literal: The Emperor finally shifted them, causing the Great King to obtain the substance of three counties—very generous.
sentences.idiomatic: In the end he went through with the transfer and let you keep the revenue of three counties instead—a remarkably generous settlement.

sentences.chinese: 大王以未嘗與皇帝相見，求入朝見，未畢昆弟之歡，而殺列侯以自為名。
sentences.literal: The Great King, because he had never met the Emperor, sought to enter court and have audience; before the joy of brotherhood was completed, he killed a full marquis to make a name for himself.
sentences.idiomatic: You asked leave to visit the capital because you had never met your imperial brother face to face, yet before you could enjoy a true reunion you murdered a marquis of the first rank to win notoriety.

sentences.chinese: 皇帝不使吏與其間，赦大王，甚厚。
sentences.literal: The Emperor did not send clerks to involve themselves in the matter and pardoned the Great King—very generous.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor declined to set the law on you and granted a full pardon—again, extraordinary indulgence.

sentences.chinese: 漢法，二千石缺，輒言漢補，大王逐漢所置，而請自置相、二千石。
sentences.literal: By Han law, when a two-thousand-dan post is vacant, one always reports to Han to fill it; the Great King drove out those Han had appointed and requested to appoint himself chancellor and two-thousand-dan officials.
sentences.idiomatic: Statute requires that vacancies among the two-thousand-dan officials be filled from the center; you expelled the men the court had posted and demanded the right to name your own chancellor and senior officers.

sentences.chinese: 皇帝骫天下正法而許大王，甚厚。
sentences.literal: The Emperor bent the correct law of all under Heaven and granted the Great King—very generous.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor strained the law of the empire to grant even that—a depth of favor you can hardly repay.

sentences.chinese: 大王欲屬國為布衣，守塚真定。
sentences.literal: The Great King wished to hand over the state and become a commoner, guarding the tomb at Zhending.
sentences.idiomatic: You asked to surrender your kingdom, live as a commoner, and tend your mother's grave in Zhending.

sentences.chinese: 皇帝不許，使大王毋失南面之尊，甚厚。
sentences.literal: The Emperor did not permit it, causing the Great King not to lose the honor of facing south—very generous.
sentences.idiomatic: He would not hear of it and insisted that you keep the dignity of a ruling prince—yet another mark of his kindness.

sentences.chinese: 大王宜日夜奉法度，修貢職，以稱皇帝之厚德，今乃輕言恣行，以負謗於天下，甚非計也。
sentences.literal: The Great King ought day and night to uphold the laws and regulations, cultivate tribute duties, thereby to match the Emperor's thick virtue; now then lightly speaking and wantonly acting, thereby to bear slander from all under Heaven—is very much not a plan.
sentences.idiomatic: You should be observing the statutes and sending tribute without fail, day and night, to show gratitude for such patience. Instead you speak rashly and act as you please, inviting the censure of the whole realm. That is no way to secure your position.

sentences.chinese: 夫大王以千里為宅居，以萬民為臣妾，此高皇帝之厚德也。
sentences.literal: Now the Great King takes a thousand li as his dwelling and the myriad people as his concubines and servants—this is the thick virtue of the Supreme Emperor.
sentences.idiomatic: You hold a thousand li as your demesne and count tens of thousands as your subjects—that is the legacy the Gaozu Emperor bequeathed you out of deep kindness.

sentences.chinese: 高帝蒙霜露，沫風雨，赴矢石，野戰攻城，身被創痍，以為子孫成萬世之業，艱難危苦甚矣，大王不思先帝之艱苦，日夜怵惕，修身正行，養犧牲，豐潔粢盛，奉祭祀，以無忘先帝之功德，而欲屬國為布衣，甚過。
sentences.literal: The Supreme Emperor endured frost and dew, licked wind and rain, went to arrows and stones, fought in the field and assaulted cities, his body bearing wounds and scars, in order for sons and grandsons to complete the enterprise of ten thousand generations—the hardship and peril and bitterness were extreme; the Great King does not ponder the Supreme Emperor's hardship, day and night fearful and cautious, cultivating the person and correcting conduct, raising sacrificial victims, making abundant and pure grain offerings, serving sacrifices, thereby not to forget the Supreme Emperor's merit and virtue, yet wishes to hand over the state and be a commoner—very excessive.
sentences.idiomatic: The Gaozu Emperor braved wind and weather, faced arrows and siege engines, and bore scars on his body to win an empire that would endure for your line. The cost was beyond measure. You ought to tremble day and night, mend your conduct, fatten the sacrificial beasts, prepare spotless offerings, and keep his shrines bright in memory of what he did. To throw away your throne for a commoner's life is to betray that sacrifice in the gravest way.

sentences.chinese: 且夫貪讓國土之名，輕廢先帝之業，不可以言孝。
sentences.literal: Moreover coveting the name of yielding state territory, lightly casting aside the Supreme Emperor's enterprise—one cannot call this filial.
sentences.idiomatic: To covet the reputation of humility while casting aside what your father built is not filial piety by any measure.

sentences.chinese: 父為之基，而不能守，不賢。
sentences.literal: The father made for him the foundation, yet he cannot guard it—not worthy.
sentences.idiomatic: Your father laid the foundation; you cannot hold it—that is not the conduct of a worthy man.

sentences.chinese: 不求守長陵，而求之真定，先母后父，不誼。
sentences.literal: Not seeking to guard Changling, yet seeking it at Zhending—mother first, father after—not right.
sentences.idiomatic: You would not stay to guard your father's tomb at Changling but insist on Zhending for your mother's sake—putting mother before father is contrary to right principle.

sentences.chinese: 數逆天子之令，不順。
sentences.literal: Repeatedly opposing the Son of Heaven's commands—not compliant.
sentences.idiomatic: Time and again you have defied the emperor's orders—that is disloyalty.

sentences.chinese: 言節行以高兄，無禮。
sentences.literal: Speaking of integrity of conduct to elevate the elder brother—without ritual.
sentences.idiomatic: You parade your moral outrage to shame your elder brother—that is rank discourtesy.

sentences.chinese: 幸臣有罪，大者立斷，小者肉刑，不仁。
sentences.literal: Favored ministers having guilt, great ones immediately cut off, small ones mutilating punishment—not benevolent.
sentences.idiomatic: When a favorite commits a crime, you execute him on the spot if the offense is grave or mutilate him if it is slight—such cruelty is the opposite of benevolence.

sentences.chinese: 貴布衣一劍之任，賤王侯之位，不知。
sentences.literal: Honoring the single-sword duty of a commoner, despising the position of kings and marquises—not knowing.
sentences.idiomatic: You prize the bravado of a swordsman in plain cloth and scorn the station of a prince—that is folly, not wisdom.

sentences.chinese: 不好學問大道，觸情忘行，不祥。
sentences.literal: Not liking to study and inquire into the great Way, touching emotion and forgetting conduct—not auspicious.
sentences.idiomatic: You spurn learning and the great principles, follow every impulse, and forget how a ruler should act—such conduct invites misfortune.

sentences.chinese: 此八者，危亡之路也，而大王行之，棄南面之位，奮諸、賁之勇，常出入危亡之路，臣之所見，高皇帝之神必不廟食於大王之手，明白。
sentences.literal: These eight are roads of peril and destruction, yet the Great King walks them, casting aside the position of facing south, rousing the bravery of Zhu and Ben, constantly going out and in on roads of peril and destruction—as your servant sees it, the spirit of the Supreme Emperor will surely not receive temple offerings from the Great King's hands—clear and bright.
sentences.idiomatic: These eight faults are the path to ruin, yet you pursue them: you abandon your throne to play the hero like Zhu Hai or Meng Ben, and you tread the brink of disaster at every turn. In my view the spirit of the Gaozu Emperor will never accept sacrifice from your house if you continue on this course—that much is plain.

sentences.chinese: 昔者，周公誅管叔，放蔡叔，以安周；
sentences.literal: In former times, the Duke of Zhou executed Guan Shu and banished Cai Shu, thereby to pacify Zhou;
sentences.idiomatic: In antiquity the Duke of Zhou executed his brother Guan Shu and banished Cai Shu to secure the Zhou house;

sentences.chinese: 齊桓殺其弟，以反國；
sentences.literal: Duke Huan of Qi killed his younger brother, thereby to return to the state;
sentences.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi put his brother to death in order to regain his throne;

sentences.chinese: 秦始皇殺兩弟，遷其母，以安秦；
sentences.literal: The First Emperor of Qin killed two younger brothers, banished his mother, thereby to pacify Qin;
sentences.idiomatic: The First Emperor killed two rebellious brothers and removed his mother from court to stabilize Qin;

sentences.chinese: 頃王亡代，高帝奪之國，以便事；
sentences.literal: King Qing lost Dai; the Supreme Emperor seized his state, thereby to facilitate affairs;
sentences.idiomatic: When the king of Dai lost his realm, Gaozu annexed it for the good of the realm;

sentences.chinese: 濟北舉兵，皇帝誅之，以安漢。
sentences.literal: The King of Jinbei raised troops; the Emperor executed him, thereby to pacify Han.
sentences.idiomatic: When the king of Jinbei rose in arms, the emperor struck him down to restore peace to Han.

sentences.chinese: 故周、齊行之於古，秦、漢用之於今，大王不察古今之所以安國便事，而欲以親戚之意望於太上，不可得也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Zhou and Qi practiced it in antiquity, Qin and Han employ it in the present; the Great King does not examine how antiquity and the present thereby pacify the state and facilitate affairs, yet wishes by kin feeling's intent to look to the Grand Supreme—cannot be obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou and Qi did as much in the old days; Qin and Han do the same today. You ignore how rulers past and present secure their states, yet imagine that blood ties alone will move the sovereign to spare you—that is a vain hope.

sentences.chinese: 亡之諸侯，游宦事人，及捨匿者，論皆有法。
sentences.literal: Fugitive feudal lords, wandering officials serving others, and those who harbor and conceal—all are judged by statute.
sentences.idiomatic: Deserted nobles, wandering retainers in others' service, and anyone who harbors them are all covered by explicit law.

sentences.chinese: 其在王所，吏主者坐。
sentences.literal: If they are at the king's place, the supervising official is held liable.
sentences.idiomatic: When such men turn up on a prince's lands, the officials in charge bear the penalty.

sentences.chinese: 今諸侯子為吏者，御史主；
sentences.literal: Now for sons of feudal lords who become clerks, the imperial clerk supervises;
sentences.idiomatic: Sons of nobles who take office as clerks answer to the imperial clerk;

sentences.chinese: 為軍吏者，中尉主；
sentences.literal: those who become military clerks, the commandant of the capital supervises;
sentences.idiomatic: those who serve as military officers, to the commandant of the capital;

sentences.chinese: 客出入殿門者，衛尉大行主；
sentences.literal: guests who go in and out of the palace hall gates, the commandant of the guards and the grand messenger supervise;
sentences.idiomatic: guests who pass the palace gates fall under the commandant of the guards and the grand messenger;

sentences.chinese: 諸從蠻夷來歸誼及以亡名數自佔者，內史縣令主。
sentences.literal: all who follow barbarians coming to submit in good faith and those who by flight from registered names self-register—the imperial secretary and county magistrates supervise.
sentences.idiomatic: tribesmen who come to offer allegiance and fugitives who surrender themselves are registered by the metropolitan governor and county magistrates.

sentences.chinese: 相欲委下吏，無與其禍，不可得也。
sentences.literal: If the chancellor wishes to shift blame to lower clerks and not share in the calamity—cannot be obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: Your chancellor cannot shift the blame downward and hope to escape the consequences himself.

sentences.chinese: 王若不改，漢系大王邸，論相以下，為之奈何？
sentences.literal: If the king does not reform, Han will bind the Great King in his mansion and prosecute from the chancellor downward—what to do about it?
sentences.idiomatic: If you do not mend your ways, the court will seal your residence in the capital and prosecute every official from your chancellor on down. What then?

sentences.chinese: 夫墮父大業，退為布衣所哀，幸臣皆伏法而誅，為天下笑，以羞先帝之德，甚為大王不取也。
sentences.literal: Now to ruin the father's great enterprise, withdraw and be pitied by commoners, favored ministers all submit to law and are executed, be laughed at by all under Heaven, thereby to shame the Supreme Emperor's virtue—very much is this not to be taken by the Great King.
sentences.idiomatic: To squander your father's achievement, sink until commoners pity you, watch your favorites led off to execution, and become a laughingstock before the empire would disgrace the memory of Gaozu. Surely that is a course you cannot intend.

sentences.chinese: 宜急改操易行，上書謝罪，曰：「臣不幸早失先帝，少孤，呂氏之世，未嘗忘死。
sentences.literal: You ought urgently to change conduct and alter practice, submit a memorial apologizing for guilt, saying, "Your servant unhappily early lost the Supreme Emperor, was young and orphaned; in the age of the Lü clan, never forgot death.
sentences.idiomatic: You should change course at once and memorialize the throne in words such as these: "I was orphaned while still a boy and lived every day of the Lü regime expecting execution.

sentences.chinese: 陛下即位，臣怙恩德驕盈，行多不軌。
sentences.literal: When Your Majesty ascended the throne, your servant relied on favor and virtue, was arrogant and overflowing, conduct much not in the track.
sentences.idiomatic: Since Your Majesty took the throne I have presumed on your kindness, grown arrogant, and repeatedly overstepped what the law allows.

sentences.chinese: 追念罪過，恐懼，伏地待誅不敢起。」
sentences.literal: Reflecting on crimes and faults, fearful, prostrate on the ground awaiting execution, not daring to rise."
sentences.idiomatic: When I think of my offenses I tremble; I lie prostrate and await death, not daring to rise."

sentences.chinese: 皇帝聞之必喜。
sentences.literal: When the Emperor hears it he will surely be pleased.
sentences.idiomatic: Such words would please the emperor.

sentences.chinese: 大王昆弟歡欣於上，群臣皆得延壽於上；
sentences.literal: The Great King's fraternal brethren will be joyful above; the host of ministers all will obtain extended life above;
sentences.idiomatic: You and your brother would be reconciled in the Son of Heaven's sight, and every minister could look to a long career at court;

sentences.chinese: 上下得宜，海內常安。
sentences.literal: above and below obtaining what is fitting, within the seas constantly at peace.
sentences.idiomatic: high and low would be at ease, and the realm would know lasting calm.

sentences.chinese: 願孰計而疾行之。
sentences.literal: I hope you will maturely plan and swiftly practice it.
sentences.idiomatic: Weigh this carefully and act on it without delay.

sentences.chinese: 行之有疑，禍如發矢，不可追已。
sentences.literal: If in practicing it there is hesitation, calamity is like loosing an arrow—cannot be pursued afterward.
sentences.idiomatic: Hesitate, and disaster will fly like a loosed arrow—nothing can call it back.

sentences.chinese: 王得書不說。
sentences.literal: The king received the letter and was not pleased.
sentences.idiomatic: The king read the letter and took no pleasure in it.

sentences.chinese: 六年，令男子但等七十人與棘蒲侯柴武太子奇謀，以輦車四十乘反谷口，令人使閩越、匈奴。
sentences.literal: In the sixth year, he ordered the man Dan and others, seventy persons, together with Qi, heir of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, to plot, using forty convoy wagons to rebel at Gukou, and sent men to envoy Minyue and the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: In the sixth year of Wen's reign he had seventy men under a certain Dan join Qi, son of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, in a plot to seize Gukou with forty wagonloads of followers and to send envoys to Minyue and the Xiongnu.

sentences.chinese: 事覺，治之，乃使使召淮南王。
sentences.literal: The affair was discovered; they prosecuted it; thereupon they sent envoys to summon the King of Huainan.
sentences.idiomatic: When the plot came to light and the principals were arrested, the court sent messengers to summon the king of Huainan to the capital.

sentences.chinese: 王至長安，丞相張蒼，典客馮敬行御史大夫事，與宗正、廷尉雜奏：「長廢先帝法，不聽天子詔，居處無度，為黃屋蓋擬天子，擅為法令，不用法令。
sentences.literal: When the king arrived at Chang'an, Chancellor Zhang Cang, the protocol secretary Feng Jing acting the office of imperial counselor, together with the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice jointly memorialized: "Chang casts aside the Supreme Emperor's laws, does not heed the Son of Heaven's edicts, dwelling and deportment without measure, used a yellow canopy emulating the Son of Heaven, on his own authority made laws and orders, does not use the laws and orders."
sentences.idiomatic: When Liu Chang reached Chang'an, Chancellor Zhang Cang, Feng Jing of the office for guests (acting as imperial counselor), the director of the imperial clan, and the commandant of justice filed a joint indictment: "Liu Chang has spurned the laws left by the late emperor, defied imperial edicts, and lived without restraint. He has used the yellow imperial canopy, issued laws on his own authority, and set aside the statutes of Han."

sentences.chinese: 及所置吏，以其郎中春為丞相，收聚漢諸侯人及有罪亡者，匿為居，為治家室，賜與財物、爵祿、田宅，爵或至關內侯，奉以二千石所當得。
sentences.literal: Moreover among officials he appointed, taking his gentleman-of-the-palace Chun as chancellor, he gathered Han feudal lords' people and those guilty who had fled, concealed them as residents, for them built houses and chambers, bestowed goods and wealth, ranks and salaries, fields and dwellings; ranks sometimes reaching marquis within the passes, supplying them with what two-thousand-dan officials ought to receive.
sentences.idiomatic: Among his appointees he made his retainer Chun chancellor, harbored runaways and criminals from other nobles' households, hid them in his domain, built them houses, and showered them with wealth, titles, fields, and stipends—some to the rank of marquis within the passes, all on the scale reserved for two-thousand-dan officials of the court.

sentences.chinese: 大夫但、士伍開章等七十人與棘蒲侯太子奇謀反，欲以危宗廟社稷，謀使閩越及匈奴發其兵。
sentences.literal: Grandee Dan, common soldier Kaizhang and others seventy persons together with the heir of Marquis Jipu Qi plotted rebellion, intending thereby to endanger the ancestral temple and altars of soil and grain, plotting to cause Minyue and the Xiongnu to raise their troops.
sentences.idiomatic: Grandee Dan, the discharged soldier Kaizhang, and some seventy confederates conspired with Qi, heir of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, to subvert the altars of state and to rouse Minyue and the Xiongnu to arms.

sentences.chinese: 事覺，長安尉奇等往捕開章，長匿不予，與故中尉□忌謀，殺以閉口，為棺槨衣衾，葬之肥陵，謾吏曰『不知安在』。
sentences.literal: When the affair was discovered, the Chang'an commandant Qi and others went to arrest Kaizhang; Chang concealed and did not hand him over, plotted with the former commandant [missing graph] Ji, killed him to close mouths, made coffin and outer coffin, shroud and quilt, buried him at Feiling, deceived the clerks saying "do not know where he is."
sentences.idiomatic: When the plot was uncovered, the Chang'an commandant Qi went to seize Kaizhang, but the king hid him and refused to surrender him. He then plotted with his former commandant [name missing] Ji to murder Kaizhang and silence him, gave him a full burial at Feiling, and told the officials he had no idea where the man had gone.

sentences.chinese: 又陽聚土，樹表其上曰『開章死，葬此下』。
sentences.literal: Moreover falsely he piled earth, erected a marker on it saying "Kaizhang died, buried below this."
sentences.idiomatic: He then piled up a mound of earth and set a post on top falsely declaring, "Kaizhang is dead and lies buried beneath this spot.

sentences.chinese: 及長身自賊殺無罪者一人；
sentences.literal: Moreover Chang personally murdered by violence one guiltless person;
sentences.idiomatic: He personally murdered one innocent man with his own hands.

sentences.chinese: 令吏論殺無罪者六人；
sentences.literal: he ordered clerks to judge and kill six guiltless persons;
sentences.idiomatic: he ordered his officials to condemn and execute six more innocents.

sentences.chinese: 為亡命棄市詐捕命者以除罪；
sentences.literal: for fugitives under sentence of execution in the market he falsely arrested others who were wanted, thereby to remove their guilt;
sentences.idiomatic: for men facing execution as fugitives, he staged sham roundups of other wanted men so their capital crimes could be wiped away.

sentences.chinese: 擅罪人，無告劾繫治城旦以上十四人；
sentences.literal: he on his own authority imprisoned men, without accusation or impeachment bound and treated fourteen persons at hard labor dawn watch and above;
sentences.idiomatic: without indictment he jailed fourteen men and sentenced them to hard labor or worse.

sentences.chinese: 赦免罪人死罪十八人，城旦春以下五十八人；
sentences.literal: he pardoned and released convicts of capital crime eighteen persons, of hard labor dawn and spring pounding and below fifty-eight persons;
sentences.idiomatic: he pardoned eighteen men condemned to death and fifty-eight serving lesser hard-labor sentences.

sentences.chinese: 賜人爵關內侯以下九十四人。
sentences.literal: he bestowed on people ranks from marquis within the passes downward ninety-four persons.
sentences.idiomatic: he ennobled ninety-four followers with ranks as high as marquis within the passes.

sentences.chinese: 前日長病，陛下心憂之，使使者賜棗脯，長不肯見拜使者。
sentences.literal: The other day Chang was ill; Your Majesty's heart worried for him, sent envoys to bestow jujubes and dried meat; Chang was unwilling to appear and bow to the envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: When he fell ill not long ago Your Majesty sent him dates and dried meat by messenger, yet he refused to receive the envoys or acknowledge the gift.

sentences.chinese: 南海民處廬江界中者反，淮南吏卒擊之。
sentences.literal: People of Nanhai dwelling within the border of Lujiang rebelled; Huainan clerks and soldiers struck them.
sentences.idiomatic: When Nanhai tribesmen living along the Lujiang frontier rose in revolt, he sent Huainan troops against them.

sentences.chinese: 陛下遣使者繼帛五千匹，以賜吏卒勞苦者。
sentences.literal: Your Majesty dispatched envoys with five thousand bolts of silk to bestow on clerks and soldiers who had toiled in hardship.
sentences.idiomatic: Your Majesty then sent five thousand bolts of silk to reward the officers and men who had borne the brunt of the fighting.

sentences.chinese: 長不欲受賜，謾曰『無勞苦者』。
sentences.literal: Chang did not wish to receive the gift, deceived saying "there are none who toiled in hardship."
sentences.idiomatic: Liu Chang would not accept the bounty and lied that no one deserved a reward for hardship.

sentences.chinese: 南海王織上書獻璧帛皇帝，忌擅燔其書，不以聞。
sentences.literal: The King of Nanhai Zhi submitted a memorial presenting jade disks and silks to the Emperor; Ji on his own authority burned his memorial, did not report it.
sentences.idiomatic: When the king of Nanhai, Zhi, memorialized the throne offering jade and silk, a man named Ji seized the document and burned it without informing the court.

sentences.chinese: 吏請召治忌，長不遣，謾曰『忌病』。
sentences.literal: Clerks requested to summon and prosecute Ji; Chang did not send him, deceived saying "Ji is ill."
sentences.idiomatic: When officials asked to arrest Ji for questioning, the king refused to hand him over and claimed he was too ill to travel.

sentences.chinese: 長所犯不軌，當棄市，臣請論如法」。
sentences.literal: What Chang has committed is not in the track; he ought to be executed in the market; your servants request to judge according to law."
sentences.idiomatic: The offenses Liu Chang has committed lie outside the bounds of the law; he deserves execution in the public market. We ask that he be sentenced accordingly.

sentences.chinese: 制曰：「朕不忍置法於王，其與列侯、吏二千石議。」
sentences.literal: The edict said: "We cannot bear to apply the law to the king; let him consult with full marquises and officials of two thousand dan."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor replied, "I cannot bring myself to judge my brother by the full rigor of the law. Let the marquises and senior officials deliberate."

sentences.chinese: 列侯、吏二千石臣嬰等四十三人議，皆曰：「宜論如法。」
sentences.literal: Full marquises and officials of two thousand dan, ministers Ying and others forty-three persons deliberated, all saying: "Ought to be judged according to law."
sentences.idiomatic: Forty-three marquises and two-thousand-dan officials, led by Zhou Ying, unanimously recommended that the statutes be applied as written.

sentences.chinese: 制曰：「其赦長死罪，廢勿王。」
sentences.literal: The edict said: "Pardon Chang's capital crime; depose him, do not let him be king."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor decreed, "Spare Liu Chang execution, but strip him of his kingdom and his title."

sentences.chinese: 有司奏：「請處蜀嚴道邛郵，遣其子、子母從居，縣為築蓋家室，皆日三食，給薪菜鹽炊食器席蓐。」
sentences.literal: The responsible officials memorialized: "We request to place him at the Qiongyi post in Yandao, Shu, send his sons and the mothers of his sons to follow and dwell with him, have the county build houses and roofs for them, all three meals per day, and supply firewood, vegetables, salt, cooking gear, mats, and bedding."
sentences.idiomatic: The ministry proposed banishing him to the Qiongyi relay in Yandao, Shu, allowing his sons and their mothers to go with him, ordering the county to erect proper quarters, provide three meals a day, and issue fuel, rations, salt, utensils, and bedding.

sentences.chinese: 制曰：』食長，給肉日五斤，酒二斗。
sentences.literal: The edict said: "Feed Chang; supply meat five jin a day, wine two dou."
sentences.idiomatic: The edict added, "See that he is fed generously—five jin of meat and two dou of wine each day."

sentences.chinese: 令故美人、材人得幸者十人從居。」
sentences.literal: Order ten former beauties and talented women who had received favor to follow residence."
sentences.idiomatic: Let ten of his former concubines and palace women who once shared his favor go with him into exile.

sentences.chinese: 於是盡誅所與謀者。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they executed to the last all who had plotted with him.
sentences.idiomatic: Every man implicated in the conspiracy was then put to death.

sentences.chinese: 乃遣長，載以輜車，令縣次傳。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they sent off Chang, carrying him in a curtained cart, ordering counties to relay in sequence.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu Chang was placed in a sealed prison wagon and sent west under escort, relayed from county to county along the route.
